name: Křivoklát Castle
region: Central Bohemia, Křivoklátsko forests, 50km west of Prague
architectural_style: Royal Bohemian Gothic castle with 19th-century Fürstenberg restoration — the castle occupies a spur above the Berounka River at the edge of the Křivoklátsko forest reserve; the principal building phases are the 13th-century Přemyslid royal expansion under Otakar II, Wenceslaus IV's late-14th-century rebuilding after fire damage, and the definitive Gothic transformation under Vladislaus II Jagiellon (1471–1516) who added the ribbed-vault Gothic chapel, the library wing, and the great residential hall; a severe fire in 1826 destroyed large portions of the castle, and the subsequent Fürstenberg family restoration (1826 onward) re-roofed the ruins in a historicist manner, preserving authentic late-Gothic fabric in the chapel and library while rebuilding the outer buildings in a 19th-century Gothic Revival mode; the result is characteristic of Central European royal castles that survived as working estates rather than as pure archaeological sites

opening_hours.seasonal_note: Hours and tour availability vary by season; the castle closes Mondays year-round. Visits are by guided tour only — the Czech National Heritage Institute runs several tour circuits of different lengths and access levels. Confirm current schedule at hradkrivoklat.cz. The GYG private day trip from Prague (t1365619, from $283) is listed as a new activity with no reviews yet; it includes entrance fees and a tour guide at the castle.

visit_duration: 1.5–2 hours (standard guided tour); full day (GYG private day trip from Prague)
best_season: May to September — the Křivoklátsko forests surrounding the castle are at their most atmospheric in late spring and early summer; the castle's hilltop setting is best appreciated in clear weather
accessibility: Křivoklát is a medieval castle on a hillside; the approach involves uphill walking on cobbled surfaces. Interior access is by guided tour on multi-level stairs. Not fully accessible for visitors with limited mobility.
tagline: The Bohemian kings' favorite hunting castle for four centuries — the Gothic chapel Vladislaus Jagiellon built here rivals anything in Prague, 50km away
highlights: Seven centuries of royal hunting residence — Křivoklát was the preferred hunting retreat of the Přemyslid, Luxemburg, Jagiellon, and Habsburg dynasties in succession; the Křivoklátsko forests surrounding it are among the densest preserved oak-and-beech woodland in Central Bohemia, now a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, and the reason every Bohemian king kept returning to this castle rather than to the more comfortable palaces closer to Prague

The Gothic chapel of Vladislaus II Jagiellon — built during the castle's 15th-century golden age, the ribbed-vault chapel contains carved stone altarpiece figures and a remarkable late-Gothic interior considered among the finest examples of Jagiellonian Gothic in Bohemia; the Jagiellon dynasty built in this manner across their domains from Cracow to Buda, and Křivoklát's chapel places Central Bohemia within that broader royal architectural program

One of the oldest library rooms in Bohemia — the library wing added during the Vladislaus II period contains one of the earliest surviving dedicated library spaces in Bohemia; the castle's collection of Gothic panel paintings and rare books makes it as much a museum of medieval Bohemian culture as a military monument

Wenceslaus IV's imprisonment in his own castle — King Václav IV of Bohemia (Holy Roman Emperor Wenceslaus, r. 1378–1419), who rebuilt Křivoklát as one of his preferred residences, was captured by a faction of Bohemian nobility and briefly held prisoner here in 1394 — an extraordinary reversal in which the king who had made the castle a royal seat was confined within its own walls by his own subjects

Edward Kelley's imprisonment — the English alchemist Edward Kelley, who had worked alongside John Dee at the court of Rudolf II in Prague in the 1580s, fell from imperial favor and was imprisoned at Křivoklát by Rudolf II around 1591; the castle's use as a state prison extended across several centuries and its list of captives forms a minor history of Central European political misfortune
GYG private day trip from Prague covering Křivoklát Castle + Beroun (t1365619, new activity, from $283, 9 hours, includes castle entrance fee and tour guide)
description:
Křivoklát sits at the edge of the Křivoklátsko — the dense oak-and-beech forest reserve that stretches westward from the Berounka River into Central Bohemia — and the forest is as much the point of the castle as the castle itself. Every Bohemian king who chose Křivoklát as a favorite residence chose it because of what surrounded it: the richest hunting ground within a half-day's ride of Prague, dense enough and remote enough to provide the combination of sport and retreat that medieval royal courts required. The castle and the forest shaped each other across four centuries of royal occupation, and today the Křivoklátsko is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve — an unintended conservation legacy of the centuries in which royal hunting rights kept the forest intact.

The earliest Přemyslid settlement on the spur above the Berounka dates to the 12th century, when the founding of a royal hunting lodge established the site as a permanent court attachment rather than a seasonal camp. The Přemyslid dynasty — Bohemia's ruling house from the legendary founding of the dynasty to its extinction in 1306 — made Křivoklát a standard fixture of the royal itinerary, and the castle grew from a fortified lodge into a substantial military and residential structure as the 13th century progressed. The most significant Přemyslid expansion came under Přemysl Otakar II (r. 1253–1278), the 'King of Iron and Gold' whose territorial ambitions at their peak stretched from Bohemia to the Adriatic — a ruler with the resources and appetite to build seriously, and who treated Křivoklát as a major royal castle rather than a seasonal stop.

The Luxemburg dynasty succeeded the Přemyslids on the Bohemian throne after 1310, and the castle reached a new phase of significance under Wenceslaus IV (r. 1378–1419), who rebuilt it after fire damage and made it one of his most frequented residences. Wenceslaus was a complex figure — Holy Roman Emperor as well as King of Bohemia, frequently at odds with the Bohemian nobility and ultimately deposed from the imperial throne in 1400 — and Křivoklát's place in his story includes one of the more pointed reversals in medieval Bohemian history: in 1394, a faction of the nobility captured the king and held him prisoner at Křivoklát, the very castle he had rebuilt as a royal seat. The king imprisoned in his own favorite palace by the subjects of that palace.

The castle's architectural golden age came under the Jagiellon dynasty. Vladislaus II Jagiellon (r. 1471–1516) was King of Bohemia and later also King of Hungary, ruler of a domain that stretched from the Baltic to the Balkans and whose court produced the last great flowering of Gothic architecture in Central Europe — the Jagiellonian Gothic, recognizable from Cracow's Wawel Castle to Buda's royal palace and here at Křivoklát. Under Vladislaus, the castle received the Gothic chapel whose ribbed vaulting and carved stone altarpiece figures are considered among the finest Jagiellonian Gothic interiors in Bohemia; the library wing, housing one of the oldest dedicated library spaces in the country; and the great residential hall. These are the rooms that made Křivoklát, in contemporary accounts, one of the most magnificent royal residences in Central Europe.

The castle's later history is defined by two uses: as a state prison and as a victim of fire. Royal and imperial prisoners were held here across the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries — the list includes nobility, clerics, and the English alchemist Edward Kelley, who had worked alongside John Dee at Rudolf II's Prague court in the 1580s, contributed to the emperor's obsessive alchemical research program, and fell from favor around 1591. Rudolf II imprisoned Kelley at Křivoklát, where he died following an escape attempt. The castle's prison function, like its hunting function, made it useful long after its front-line military role had ended.

A severe fire in 1826 destroyed large sections of the castle and forced a decisive intervention. The Austrian imperial authorities sold the ruin to Karl Egon II, Prince of Fürstenberg, who undertook the comprehensive reconstruction that defined the castle's present appearance — re-roofing the burned-out sections, restoring the outer buildings in a Gothic Revival manner consistent with the surviving medieval fabric, and preserving the authentic late-Gothic chapel and library while rebuilding the residential portions around them. The Fürstenbergs held the castle until 1929, when it passed to the new Czechoslovak state.

Today the castle is administered by the Czech National Heritage Institute and visited by guided tour. The collections include late-Gothic panel paintings — among the most significant medieval painting collections in any Czech castle outside Prague — hunting weapons accumulated across the centuries of royal occupation, and the rare books of the library. [Karlštejn Castle](/castles/czech-republic/karlstejn-castle), 30 kilometres east in the Berounka valley, was built by Emperor Charles IV specifically to house the Crown Jewels and imperial relics — a companion to Křivoklát in the Central Bohemian castle landscape and reachable on the same day. [Prague Castle](/castles/czech-republic/prague-castle), 50 kilometres east, is the seat of the Bohemian kings whose hunting at Křivoklát was the other half of their lives.
history: 12th century: Přemyslid princes establish a royal hunting lodge on the spur above the Berounka River — the founding of what becomes Křivoklát Castle. 13th century: Major expansion under Přemysl Otakar II (r. 1253–1278); castle becomes a substantial royal military and residential structure. 1310: Luxemburg dynasty succeeds Přemyslids as Bohemian rulers. Late 14th century: Wenceslaus IV (r. 1378–1419) rebuilds after fire; makes Křivoklát a primary residence. 1394: Wenceslaus IV captured by Bohemian nobility and briefly imprisoned at his own castle. 1471–1516: Vladislaus II Jagiellon's golden age — Gothic chapel, library wing, and great hall built; castle reaches architectural peak. 16th century: Used as royal prison; English alchemist Edward Kelley imprisoned here by Rudolf II c.1591. 1826: Severe fire destroys large portions; Austrian imperial authorities sell the ruin to Prince Karl Egon II of Fürstenberg. 1826 onward: Fürstenberg family undertakes major Gothic Revival restoration. 1929: Castle transferred to the Czechoslovak state. Present day: Administered by the Czech National Heritage Institute; open for guided tours.
how_to_visit:
**Direct visit (guided tours only):** Visits are by guided tour — the castle does not allow free-roam access. Several tour circuits are offered covering different parts of the castle at different price points. Check current schedules and prices at hradkrivoklat.cz. The castle is closed Mondays.

**Getting there:** From Prague, approximately 50km west (1 hour by car). By train: Prague Masarykovo nádraží to Křivoklát (with a change at Beroun); the journey takes about 1.5 hours. The station is a 10-minute walk from the castle.

**GYG private day trip from Prague (~$283, t1365619, 9 hours, new activity):** Includes castle entrance fees and a tour guide at the castle, plus a stop in Beroun. Departure from Prague.

**Combine with:** Karlštejn Castle (30km east in the same Berounka valley, reachable on the same day).

faqs.question: Who was Edward Kelley and why was he imprisoned here?
faqs.answer: Edward Kelley (1555–1597) was an English occultist and self-described spirit medium who worked alongside John Dee — astrologer to Elizabeth I — at the court of Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II in Prague in the 1580s. Rudolf was obsessed with alchemy and the occult, and Kelley claimed the ability to transmute base metals into gold. When Kelley's promises outpaced his results, Rudolf lost patience and imprisoned him at Křivoklát around 1591. Kelley died following an escape attempt from the castle — in some accounts by a fall while attempting to climb down from the walls. His story is one of the more vivid episodes in Rudolf II's famous Prague court, which also attracted Tycho Brahe and Johannes Kepler.

faqs.question: Why was the Křivoklátsko forest so important to Czech kings?
faqs.answer: The Křivoklátsko — now a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve — is one of the largest and most diverse temperate forest complexes in Central Bohemia, combining oak, beech, and mixed woodland in terrain that was ideal for royal deer and boar hunting. The forest's preservation as a hunting ground across centuries of royal ownership is the direct reason it survives in its present state: royal hunting rights excluded settlement and clearing, preserving the ecosystem that would otherwise have been converted to agriculture. The castle and the forest are ecologically inseparable — each owed its character to the other.
